1.description: Consumption growth and market return. This figure plots the market return (solid black), consumption growth based on the “Reported” NIPA measure (dashed), “P-J” (dashed o), “Q4-Q4” (dotted), “Garbage” (dashed diamond), and “Unfiltered” (dashed +). The garbage consumption growth is from Savov (2011). The other series are from Kroencke (2017) for the period 1960 to 2014 (yearly data).

2.description: Rejection frequencies ofH0:B=0at the 5% level using Wald andFtests. This figure plots the sizes of rank tests (Wald (solid),F(dashed)) used to testH0:B=0at the 5% level, whereBis the centered version of β (i.e., subtract theNthelement). The data are generated from the linear factor modelRt=ιNλ0+β(ft+λf)+ut, where β is set toιN·d, withd∈[−3,3]. λ0,λf, and the variances offtandutare calibrated usingN=31or the firstN=5test assets in Kroencke (2017) and the garbage consumption growth measure of Savov (2011). The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000. Similar simulation outcomes for multifactor models are presented in the Internet Appendix.

3.description: Power curves of thet-test. This figure plots power curves of thet-test with the Shanken (1992) correction. The null hypothesis isλf=2. The significance level is 5%. The DGP is calibrated to the test assets in Kroencke (2017), with “Reported,” “P-J,” “Q4-Q4,” “Garbage,” “Unfiltered,” and “Rm” as the risk factors. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000, withN=31andT=55.

4.description: Power curves of the GRS-FAR Test. This figures plots power curves of the GRS-FAR test with χ2critical values (solid),Fcritical values (dashed), and thet-test (dashed diamond) with the Shanken (1992) correction. The null hypothesis isλf=2. The significance level is 5%. The DGP is calibrated toN=31or the firstN=5test assets in Kroencke (2017) and the garbage consumption growth in Savov (2011). The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.

5.description: p-Values of testing the risk premium by the GRS-FAR test with 31 portfolio returns. The test assets, taken from Kroencke (2017), comprise the 30 portfolios sorted by size, value, and investment, plus the market portfolio, in 1960 to 2014 (yearly data). The null hypothesis is thatλfis equal to the value on the horizontal axis. The correspondingp-values of the GRS-FAR test (usingFcritical values (dashed)) are plotted, together with the 5% line (solid).

6.description: Frequency of occurrence of empty, unbounded, and bounded 95% confidence sets for the risk premia from the (subset) GRS-FAR test asTincreases. The figure plots the frequency of occurrence of empty (solid), unbounded (dashed diamond), and bounded (dashed) 95% confidence sets for the risk premia resulting from the (subset) GRS-FAR test as a function ofT. The DGP is a 31-dimensional joint normal distribution whose covariance matrix is calibrated to that of the returns on the 25 size and book-to-market sorted FF portfolios, the three FF factors (Rm, SMB, and HML), consumption growth, the lagged consumption-wealth ratio, and their interaction resulting from the Lettau-Ludvigson (2001) data. The mean of the returns in the DGP is calibrated to the sample average, while the mean of the factors is set to zero. Panel A uses the three FF factors as risk factors in the estimated model and Panel B uses consumption growth, the lagged consumption-wealth ratio, and their interaction as risk factors. The test assets are theN=25simulated returns on the FF portfolios. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.

7.description: Frequency of occurrence of empty, unbounded, and bounded 95% confidence sets for the risk premium from the GRS-FAR test asTincreases, single risk factor, misspecified models. This figure plots the frequency of occurrence of empty (solid), unbounded (dashed diamond), and bounded (dashed) 95% confidence sets for the risk premia resulting from the GRS-FAR test as a function ofT. The DGP is a 31-dimensional joint normal distribution whose covariance matrix is calibrated to that of the returns on the 25 size and book-to-market sorted FF portfolios, the three FF factors (Rm, SMB, and HML), consumption growth, the lagged consumption-wealth ratio, and their interaction resulting from the Lettau-Ludvigson (2001) data. The mean of the returns in the DGP is calibrated to the sample average, while the mean of the factors is set to zero. Panel A uses the market return as the single risk factor in the estimated model and Panel B uses consumption growth as the single risk factor. The test assets are theN=25simulated returns on the FF portfolios. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.

8.description: Frequency of occurrence of empty, unbounded, and bounded 95% confidence sets for the risk premia from the (subset) GRS-FAR test asTincreases, three risk factors, factor pricing imposed on DGPs. This figure plots the frequency of occurrence of empty (solid), unbounded (dashed diamond), and bounded (dashed) 95% confidence sets for the risk premia resulting from the (subset) GRS-FAR test as a function ofT. The DGP is a 31-dimensional joint normal distribution whose covariance matrix is calibrated to that of the returns on the 25 size and book-to-market sorted FF portfolios, the three FF factors (Rm, SMB, and HML), consumption growth, the lagged consumption-wealth ratio, and their interaction resulting from the Lettau-Ludvigson (2001) data. The factor pricing restriction is imposed on the mean of the test asset returns. Panel A uses the three FF factors as risk factors in the estimated model and Panel B uses consumption growth, the lagged consumption-wealth ratio, and their interaction as risk factors. The test assets are theN=25simulated returns on the FF portfolios. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.

9.description: Joint 95% confidence sets for(γ,δ). In this figure, we test for values of the relative rate of risk aversion γ within the range of [0, 300] and of the discount factor δ in [0.5, 1] using the GMM-AR test from Stock and Wright (2000). See the Internet Appendix for a detailed description of the test statistic. The moment conditions areEt[δ(Ct+1Ct)−γRm,t+1e]=0andEt[δ(Ct+1Ct)−γRf,t+1]=1. The shaded areas constitute the 95% joint confidence sets for γ and δ. The excess market return and the gross risk-free rate used for the test are for the period 1960 to 2014 (yearly data).

10.description: Power curves of GRS-FAR that testsH0:λf=2and GMM-AR that testsH0:γ=2/var(▵ct)at the 5% level, DGP: normal returns. This figure plots power curves of GMM-AR (solid) and GRS-FAR (dashed) at the 5% significance level. The moment condition imposed on the DGP isE(Rt)=ιNλ0+βλf. The null hypothesis isH0:λf=2for GRS-FAR andH0:γ=2/var(▵ct)for GMM-AR. The horizontal axes show the value ofλfused in the DGP (DGP: normal returnsin the Internet Appendix). The DGP is calibrated to the test assets and the unfiltered consumption growth from Kroencke (2017). TheN=5, 10, or 25 test assets used for calibration are taken from the 31 portfolios in their order starting from the market portfolio. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.

11.description: Power curves of GMM-AR that testsH0:γ=15and GRS-FAR that testsH0:λf=15×var(▵ct)at the 5% level. DGP: log-normal returns. This figure plots power curves of GMM-AR (solid) and GRS-FAR (dashed) at the 5% significance level. The moment condition imposed on the DGP isEt[δ(Ct+1Ct)−γ(ιN+Rt+1)]=ιNwithδ=0.95. The null hypothesis isH0:γ=15for GMM-AR andH0:λf=15×var(▵ct)for GRS-FAR. The horizontal axes show the value of γ used in the DGP (DGP: log-normal returnsin the Internet Appendix). The DGP is calibrated to the test assets and the unfiltered consumption growth from Kroencke (2017). TheN=5, 10, or 25 test assets used for calibration are taken from the 31 portfolios in their order starting from the market portfolio. The number of Monte Carlo replications is 5,000.
